You do not have to specify the ``media_type`` kwarg in the route handler function if the response should be JSON. But
if you wish to return a response other than JSON, you should specify this value. You can use
the :class:`MediaType <litestar.enums.MediaType>` enum for this purpose:

.. code-block:: python

   from litestar import MediaType, get

   @get("/resources", media_type=MediaType.TEXT)
   def retrieve_resource() -> str:
       return "The rumbling rabbit ran around the rock"

The value of the ``media_type`` kwarg affects both the serialization of response data and the generation of OpenAPI docs.
The above example will cause Litestar to serialize the response as a simple bytes string with a ``Content-Type`` header
value of ``text/plain``. It will also set the corresponding values in the OpenAPI documentation.

MediaType has the following members:

* MediaType.JSON: ``application/json``
* MediaType.MessagePack: ``application/x-msgpack``
* MediaType.TEXT: ``text/plain``
* MediaType.HTML: ``text/html``

[2] (docs/usage/responses.rst)
If you need to return other values and would like to extend serialization you can do
this :ref:`custom responses <usage/responses:Custom Responses>`.

You can also set an application media type string with the ``+json`` suffix
defined in `RFC 6839 <https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6839#section-3.1>`_
as the ``media_type`` and it will be recognized and serialized as json.

For example, you can use ``application/vnd.example.resource+json``
and it will work just like json but have the appropriate content-type header
and show up in the generated OpenAPI schema.

.. literalinclude:: /examples/responses/json_suffix_responses.py
    :language: python

MessagePack responses
+++++++++++++++++++++

In addition to JSON, Litestar offers support for the `MessagePack <https://msgpack.org/>`_
format which can be a time and space efficient alternative to JSON.

It supports all the same types as JSON serialization. To send a ``MessagePack`` response,
simply specify the media type as ``MediaType.MESSAGEPACK``\ :

Based on the documentation, you should use a **204 (No Content)** status code. [4] specifically mentions a bugfix ensuring that "the ``content-type`` header is not added for responses that do not have a body such as responses with status code ``204 (No Content)``."

Here's the solution:

```python
from litestar import delete

@delete("/resources/{resource_id}", status_code=204)
def delete_resource(resource_id: str) -> None:
    # delete logic here
    pass
```

By using `status_code=204` and returning `None`, Litestar will:
- Not add a `content-type` header
- Not include a response body
- Return an empty 204 response

This is the HTTP semantic meaning of 204 No Content—a successful response with no content to return.

**Note:** If you specifically need a 200 status code without a body, the documentation provided doesn't explicitly cover that scenario. For other status codes with no body, you may need to check the Litestar documentation on custom `Response` objects, which is referenced but not fully detailed in the provided docs [2].

Accessing the app instance
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

When extending the Litestar CLI, you will most likely need access to the loaded ``Litestar`` instance.
You can achieve this by adding the special ``app`` parameter to your CLI functions. This will cause the
``Litestar`` instance to be injected into the function whenever it is called from a click-context.

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Accessing the app instance programmatically

    import click
    from litestar import Litestar

    @click.command()
    def my_command(app: Litestar) -> None: ...

Using the `server_lifespan` hook
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Server lifespan hooks provide a way to run code before and after the *server* starts and stops. In contrast to the regular `lifespan` hooks, they only run once, even when a server starts multiple workers, whereas `lifespan` hooks would run for each individual worker.

This makes them suitable for tasks that should happen exactly once, like initializing a database.

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Using the `server_lifespan` hook

[2] (docs/tutorials/sqlalchemy/0-introduction.rst)
Introduction
------------

We start with a full script that shows how you can use SQLAlchemy with Litestar. In this app, we interact with
SQLAlchemy in the manner described by the
`SQLAlchemy documentation <https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/extensions/asyncio.html#synopsis-orm>`_, and so if you
are looking for more information about any of the SQLAlchemy code, this will be a great place to start.

You'll notice that we use a couple of Litestar features that you may not have encountered yet:

1. Management and injection of :ref:`application state <application-state>`
2. Use of a :ref:`Lifespan context manager <lifespan-context-managers>`

And we will continue to learn about other Litestar features as we work through the tutorial, such as:

1. Dependency injection
2. Plugins

The full app
============

While it may look imposing, this app only has minor behavioral differences to the previous example. It is still an app
that maintains a TODO list, that allows for adding, updating and viewing the collection of TODO items.

Question: Access to app state in CLI plugins

Hi!

I'm trying to build quite simple CLI plugin similar to [flask's shell](https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/3.0.x/shell/), that would allow us to easily play around with SQLAlchemy models & queries by using injected `app` object that already has access to DB. Our app relies on couple of start up hooks / plugins, including `SQLAlchemyInitPlugin`. Some of these hooks modify `app.state` in functions that are added to `AppConfig.lifespan` field ([ like in SQLAlchemyInitPlugin](https://github.com/jolt-org/advanced-alchemy/blob/v0.9.0/advanced_alchemy/extensions/litestar/plugins/init/plugin.py#L95)).

I might be completely wrong, but it seems these lifespan hooks are only called within [lifespan](https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/v2.8.1/litestar/app.py#L599) method of `Litestar` class (i.e. [here](https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/v2.8.1/litestar/_asgi/asgi_router.py#L168)). This method is not called / context manager it returns is never entered before the function defined as part of the CLI plugin is called, hence app state is empty in the shell that we create.

I'm wondering if someone could help me to find out if `app.lifespan` is part of the public API and whether i can simply use it on my own (see example below) 🙂 

Here is a simplified implementation of the plugin that injects `app` and `session_maker` variables into IPython terminal namespace (Ipython supports async code execution). It seems to work just fine, but i'm not sure that's a right way

```python
import asyncio
import sys
from functools import wraps

from click import Group
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.plugins import CLIPlugin
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import async_sessionmaker


def _make_coroutine(f):
    @wraps
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        return asyncio.run(f(*args, **kwargs))
    
    return wrapper
    
    
class ShellPlugin(CLIPlugin):
    def on_cli_init(self, cli: Group) -> None:
        @cli.command(name="shell", help="Start IPython repl with async support")
        @make_coroutine
        async def shell(app: Litestar) -> None:
            async with app.lifespan():
                session_maker: type[async_sessionmaker] = app.state["session_maker_class"]
                await asyncio.gather(
                    asyncio.to_thread(_start_shell, app=app, session_maker=session_maker)
                )


def _start_shell(app: Litestar, session_maker: type[async_sessionmaker]) -> None:
    # args accepted by function will be injected by IPython.embed into shell
    import IPython
    
    IPython.embed(
        using="asyncio",
        banner2="The coconut nut is a giant nut; If you eat too much, you'll get very fat",
    )
```

Cheers!

Your approach is correct. Based on the documentation, `app.lifespan()` is part of the public API.

## The Short Answer

Yes, your implementation is the right way. You need to enter the lifespan context manager to ensure startup hooks (including those from `SQLAlchemyInitPlugin`) run and populate `app.state` [4]. Your code does exactly that.

## Why Your Approach Works

[4] explicitly documents **Lifespan context managers** as a supported way to manage application startup/shutdown. The lifespan context manager ensures:

2. `app.state` gets populated with resources like your `session_maker_class`
3. Cleanup happens on exit

This is the same mechanism used internally when the server starts.

```python
import asyncio
import click

from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.plugins import CLIPlugin

class ShellPlugin(CLIPlugin):
    def on_cli_init(self, cli: click.Group) -> None:
        @cli.command(name="shell", help="Start IPython repl with async support")
        def shell(app: Litestar) -> None:
            # Run async code
            asyncio.run(self._shell_async(app))
    
    async def _shell_async(self, app: Litestar) -> None:
        async with app.lifespan():  # Enter lifespan to populate app.state
            session_maker = app.state["session_maker_class"]
            await asyncio.to_thread(self._start_repl, app, session_maker)
    
    def _start_repl(self, app: Litestar, session_maker) -> None:
        import IPython
        IPython.embed(using="asyncio")
```

Great question! Your approach is correct. Based on the documentation, `app.lifespan()` is part of the public API.

## The Short Answer

Yes, your implementation is the right way. You need to enter the lifespan context manager to ensure startup hooks (including those from `SQLAlchemyInitPlugin`) run and populate `app.state` [4]. Your code does exactly that.

## Why Your Approach Works

[4] explicitly documents **Lifespan context managers** as a supported way to manage application startup/shutdown. The lifespan context manager ensures:

1. All startup hooks run (including plugin initialization)
2. `app.state` gets populated with resources like your `session_maker_class`
3. Cleanup happens on exit

This is the same mechanism used internally when the server starts.

## A Cleaner Version

You can simplify your wrapper slightly using `click.pass_context`:

```python
import asyncio
import click
from functools import wraps
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.plugins import CLIPlugin

class ShellPlugin(CLIPlugin):
    def on_cli_init(self, cli: click.Group) -> None:
        @cli.command(name="shell", help="Start IPython repl with async support")
        def shell(app: Litestar) -> None:
            # Run async code
            asyncio.run(self._shell_async(app))
    
    async def _shell_async(self, app: Litestar) -> None:
        async with app.lifespan():  # Enter lifespan to populate app.state
            session_maker = app.state["session_maker_class"]
            await asyncio.to_thread(self._start_repl, app, session_maker)
    
    def _start_repl(self, app: Litestar, session_maker) -> None:
        import IPython
        IPython.embed(using="asyncio")
```

The key point: **`async with app.lifespan()` is the public API way to ensure all initialization runs before your CLI command accesses `app.state`.** [1][4]

raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":6826,"duration_api_ms":7774,"ttft_ms":4314,"ttft_stream_ms":1027,"time_to_request_ms":10,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Accessing Route Name in Litestar Templates

Yes, this is possible! The cleanest approach is to use a **template context processor** to automatically inject the route name into all templates.

## Solution: Template Context Processor

```python
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.template.config import TemplateConfig
from litestar.contrib.jinja import JinjaTemplateEngine

def inject_route_name(context: dict) -> dict:
    """Automatically inject route name into template context"""
    request = context.get("request")
    if request and hasattr(request, "route_handler") and request.route_handler:
        context["route_name"] = request.route_handler.name
    return context
```

# Accessing Route Name in Litestar Templates

Yes, this is possible! The cleanest approach is to use a **template context processor** to automatically inject the route name into all templates.

## Solution: Template Context Processor

```python
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.template.config import TemplateConfig
from litestar.contrib.jinja import JinjaTemplateEngine

def inject_route_name(context: dict) -> dict:
    """Automatically inject route name into template context"""
    request = context.get("request")
    if request and hasattr(request, "route_handler") and request.route_handler:
        context["route_name"] = request.route_handler.name
    return context

template_config = TemplateConfig(
    engine=JinjaTemplateEngine,
    directory="templates",
    context_processors=[inject_route_name],
)

app = Litestar(route_handlers=[...], template_config=template_config)
```

## Then in your template (no context needed):

```html
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(route_name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

## Handler (simplified):

```python
@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
    return Template(template_name='login.html')  # No manual context!
```

## Alternative: Direct access (if available)

You can also try directly in the template:
```html
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(request.route_handler.name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

But the **context processor approach is recommended** as it's more explicit and ensures availability across all templates.

The issue is with how you're using the `media_type` parameter. The `media_type` on the `@post` decorator specifies the **response** content type, not the request type. Here's the corrected approach:

## Correct Implementation

```python
        "File Name": data.filename,

        "Headers": data.headers
    }
```

3. **File type doesn't matter** — `.tar.gz`, `.zip`, etc. are handled identically as raw bytes. Litestar converts them to `UploadFile` instances regardless of the actual file type

# File Upload Implementation

The issue is with how you're using the `media_type` parameter. The `media_type` on the `@post` decorator specifies the **response** content type, not the request type. Here's the corrected approach:

## Correct Implementation

**Simplest approach:**
```python
@post("/upload")
async def upload(self, data: UploadFile) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return { 
        "File Name": data.filename,
        "Content Type": data.content_type,
        "Headers": data.headers
    }
```

**Or using the explicit shorthand** [2]:
```python
from litestar.types import MultipartBody

@post("/upload")
async def upload(self, data: MultipartBody[UploadFile]) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return { 
        "File Name": data.filename,
        "Content Type": data.content_type,
        "Headers": data.headers
    }
```

## Key Points

1. **Remove `media_type` from decorator** — this controls the response format (JSON is fine for your response), not the request parsing [1]
2. **No need for explicit `Body()` annotation** — Litestar automatically treats `UploadFile` parameters as multipart form data [1]
3. **File type doesn't matter** — `.tar.gz`, `.zip`, etc. are handled identically as raw bytes. Litestar converts them to `UploadFile` instances regardless of the actual file type

The `UploadFile` class provides convenient methods like `.read()`, `.seek()`, `.write()`, and `.rolled_to_disk()` for working with the uploaded content [5].

QUESTION:
200 Response without body and content-type

How can I make an endpoint return a Response without a body and without a content-type? Returning `None` from the route handler just gets me "application/json" media type with `null` as the body. I have looked in the documentation but I can't find anything.

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
"200 without a body" isn't really a thing. As @guacs said, if you truly want to omit the body, you should use a `204` code. However, if you're fine with sending an empty body, you could just do something like

```python
@get("/")
async def handler() -> str:
    return ""
```

which sends an empty plaintext response.



ANSWER A:
Based on the documentation, you should use a **204 (No Content)** status code. [4] specifically mentions a bugfix ensuring that "the ``content-type`` header is not added for responses that do not have a body such as responses with status code ``204 (No Content)``."

Here's the solution:

```python
from litestar import delete

@delete("/resources/{resource_id}", status_code=204)
def delete_resource(resource_id: str) -> None:
    # delete logic here
    pass
```

By using `status_code=204` and returning `None`, Litestar will:
- Not add a `content-type` header
- Not include a response body
- Return an empty 204 response

This is the HTTP semantic meaning of 204 No Content—a successful response with no content to return.

**Note:** If you specifically need a 200 status code without a body, the documentation provided doesn't explicitly cover that scenario. For other status codes with no body, you may need to check the Litestar documentation on custom `Response` objects, which is referenced but not fully detailed in the provided docs [2].

ANSWER B:
# Return Empty 200 Response

Use `Response` directly instead of returning a Python object that gets auto-serialized:

```python
from starlette.responses import Response

@app.get("/endpoint")
async def my_endpoint():
    return Response(status_code=200)
```

This returns a 200 with an empty body and no `content-type` header.

## Alternatives

If you need to be explicit about the empty content:

```python
return Response(status_code=200, content="")
```

Or explicitly set `media_type` to `None`:

```python
return Response(status_code=200, media_type=None)
```

The key is returning a `Response` object directly—this bypasses FastAPI's automatic serialization that converts `None` to `{"application/json": null}`.

You are grading two answers to a developer's question about the tool "litestar".

QUESTION:
Access to app state in CLI plugins

Hi!

I'm trying to build quite simple CLI plugin similar to [flask's shell](https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/3.0.x/shell/), that would allow us to easily play around with SQLAlchemy models & queries by using injected `app` object that already has access to DB. Our app relies on couple of start up hooks / plugins, including `SQLAlchemyInitPlugin`. Some of these hooks modify `app.state` in functions that are added to `AppConfig.lifespan` field ([ like in SQLAlchemyInitPlugin](https://github.com/jolt-org/advanced-alchemy/blob/v0.9.0/advanced_alchemy/extensions/litestar/plugins/init/plugin.py#L95)).

I might be completely wrong, but it seems these lifespan hooks are only called within [lifespan](https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/v2.8.1/litestar/app.py#L599) method of `Litestar` class (i.e. [here](https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/blob/v2.8.1/litestar/_asgi/asgi_router.py#L168)). This method is not called / context manager it returns is never entered before the function defined as part of the CLI plugin is called, hence app state is empty in the shell that we create.

I'm wondering if someone could help me to find out if `app.lifespan` is part of the public API and whether i can simply use it on my own (see example below) 🙂 

Here is a simplified implementation of the plugin that injects `app` and `session_maker` variables into IPython terminal namespace (Ipython supports async code execution). It seems to work just fine, but i'm not sure that's a right way

```python
import asyncio
import sys
from functools import wraps

from click import Group
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.plugins import CLIPlugin
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import async_sessionmaker


def _make_coroutine(f):
    @wraps
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        return asyncio.run(f(*args, **kwargs))
    
    return wrapper
    
    
class ShellPlugin(CLIPlugin):
    def on_cli_init(self, cli: Group) -> None:
        @cli.command(name="shell", help="Start IPython repl with async support")
        @make_coroutine
        async def shell(app: Litestar) -> None:
            async with app.lifespan():
                session_maker: type[async_sessionmaker] = app.state["session_maker_class"]
                await asyncio.gather(
                    asyncio.to_thread(_start_shell, app=app, session_maker=session_maker)
                )


def _start_shell(app: Litestar, session_maker: type[async_sessionmaker]) -> None:
    # args accepted by function will be injected by IPython.embed into shell
    import IPython
    
    IPython.embed(
        using="asyncio",
        banner2="The coconut nut is a giant nut; If you eat too much, you'll get very fat",
    )
```

Cheers!

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
`Litestar.lifespan()` is documented: https://docs.litestar.dev/2/reference/app.html#litestar.app.Litestar.lifespan

The lifespan is only entered by the application in response to the ASGI lifespan startup message from the server, which doesn't happen for cli operations (except `litestar run` of course).

Your approach looks reasonable to me. Are you encountering any issues?

Another way you can approach it is to have your own lifespan context that is separate from, but called from within the litestar lifespan context. This way you can enter resource contexts separate from the application.

ANSWER A:
# Accessing App State in Litestar CLI Plugins

Yes, `app.lifespan()` is part of the public API and your approach is correct! Using `async with app.lifespan()` ensures all startup hooks execute before your shell starts.

## Issues in Your Code

There are a couple of bugs to fix:

1. `@wraps` should be `@wraps(f)` 
2. You need proper access to the app instance in your Click command

## Corrected Implementation

```python
import asyncio
from functools import wraps
from click import Group
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.plugins import CLIPlugin
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import async_sessionmaker


def _make_coroutine(f):
    @wraps(f)  # ← Fix: pass the function
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        return asyncio.run(f(*args, **kwargs))
    return wrapper


class ShellPlugin(CLIPlugin):
    def on_cli_init(self, cli: Group) -> None:
        @cli.command(name="shell", help="Start IPython repl with async support")
        @_make_coroutine  # ← Fix: add parentheses
        async def shell() -> None:
            # Access app from Click context
            app: Litestar = cli.make_context("shell", []).obj
            
            async with app.lifespan():
                session_maker = app.state["session_maker_class"]
                await asyncio.to_thread(
                    _start_shell, app=app, session_maker=session_maker
                )


def _start_shell(app: Litestar, session_maker: type[async_sessionmaker]) -> None:
    import IPython
    IPython.embed(
        using="asyncio",
        banner2="Your custom banner here",
    )
```

## Key Points

- ✅ `app.lifespan()` is public API and the right way to manually trigger startup hooks
- ✅ Your approach of wrapping async CLI commands works well
- ✅ Access the app instance from the Click context (typically passed as `ctx.obj`)

This pattern is sound for building interactive development tools!

ANSWER B:
Great question! Your approach is correct. Based on the documentation, `app.lifespan()` is part of the public API.

## The Short Answer

Yes, your implementation is the right way. You need to enter the lifespan context manager to ensure startup hooks (including those from `SQLAlchemyInitPlugin`) run and populate `app.state` [4]. Your code does exactly that.

## Why Your Approach Works

[4] explicitly documents **Lifespan context managers** as a supported way to manage application startup/shutdown. The lifespan context manager ensures:

1. All startup hooks run (including plugin initialization)
2. `app.state` gets populated with resources like your `session_maker_class`
3. Cleanup happens on exit

This is the same mechanism used internally when the server starts.

## A Cleaner Version

You can simplify your wrapper slightly using `click.pass_context`:

```python
import asyncio
import click
from functools import wraps
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.plugins import CLIPlugin

class ShellPlugin(CLIPlugin):
    def on_cli_init(self, cli: click.Group) -> None:
        @cli.command(name="shell", help="Start IPython repl with async support")
        def shell(app: Litestar) -> None:
            # Run async code
            asyncio.run(self._shell_async(app))
    
    async def _shell_async(self, app: Litestar) -> None:
        async with app.lifespan():  # Enter lifespan to populate app.state
            session_maker = app.state["session_maker_class"]
            await asyncio.to_thread(self._start_repl, app, session_maker)
    
    def _start_repl(self, app: Litestar, session_maker) -> None:
        import IPython
        IPython.embed(using="asyncio")
```

The key point: **`async with app.lifespan()` is the public API way to ensure all initialization runs before your CLI command accesses `app.state`.** [1][4]

prompt:
You are grading two answers to a developer's question about the tool "litestar".

QUESTION:
Upload zip or tar.gz file in POST

I have the following to be able to upload a .tar.gz file.
`@post("/upload", media_type="application/x-gtar")
    async def upload(self,
        data: Annotated[UploadFile, Body(media_type=RequestEncodingType.MULTI_PART)]
    ) -> dict[str, Any]:
        return { 
            "File Name": data.filename,
            "Content Type": data.content_type,
            "Headers": data.headers
        }`
However, I keep getting 500 internal server errors. Am I implementing this correctly?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
`media_type` is used to serialize whatever you return from the handler `upload`. Litestar does not know how to serialize `dict[str, Any]` into a tar file which is why it fails.

`media_type` is not used to indicate that your route accepts tar gz files.

You can remove `media_type` and you will notice that you can indeed upload a file and get a json response

ANSWER A:
# File Upload Implementation

The issue is with how you're using the `media_type` parameter. The `media_type` on the `@post` decorator specifies the **response** content type, not the request type. Here's the corrected approach:

## Correct Implementation

**Simplest approach:**
```python
@post("/upload")
async def upload(self, data: UploadFile) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return { 
        "File Name": data.filename,
        "Content Type": data.content_type,
        "Headers": data.headers
    }
```

**Or using the explicit shorthand** [2]:
```python
from litestar.types import MultipartBody

@post("/upload")
async def upload(self, data: MultipartBody[UploadFile]) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return { 
        "File Name": data.filename,
        "Content Type": data.content_type,
        "Headers": data.headers
    }
```

## Key Points

1. **Remove `media_type` from decorator** — this controls the response format (JSON is fine for your response), not the request parsing [1]
2. **No need for explicit `Body()` annotation** — Litestar automatically treats `UploadFile` parameters as multipart form data [1]
3. **File type doesn't matter** — `.tar.gz`, `.zip`, etc. are handled identically as raw bytes. Litestar converts them to `UploadFile` instances regardless of the actual file type

The `UploadFile` class provides convenient methods like `.read()`, `.seek()`, `.write()`, and `.rolled_to_disk()` for working with the uploaded content [5].

ANSWER B:
# The Issues

1. **`Body()` is wrong for UploadFile** — This is the likely cause of your 500 error. `UploadFile` should use `File()`, not `Body()`.

2. **`media_type` in decorator** — This sets the *response* content type, not request. Remove it or use `"application/json"`.

3. **Unnecessary `RequestEncodingType`** — `UploadFile` handles `multipart/form-data` automatically.

# Corrected Code

```python
from fastapi import UploadFile, File
from typing import Annotated

@post("/upload")
async def upload(self, data: Annotated[UploadFile, File()]) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return { 
        "File Name": data.filename,
        "Content Type": data.content_type,
        "Headers": data.headers
    }
```

Or the simplest version:

```python
@post("/upload")
async def upload(self, data: UploadFile) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return { 
        "File Name": data.filename,
        "Content Type": data.content_type,
        "Headers": data.headers
    }
```

# To Read the File Content

If you need to process the uploaded file:

```python
@post("/upload")
async def upload(self, data: UploadFile) -> dict[str, Any]:
    contents = await data.read()  # Read file bytes
    return { 
        "File Name": data.filename,
        "Content Type": data.content_type,
        "Size": len(contents)
    }
```

The key: **`UploadFile` + `File()`**, not `Body()`.

[1] (docs/usage/templating.rst)
Passing context to the template is very simple - its one of the kwargs expected by the :class:`Template <litestar.response.Template>`
container, so simply pass a string keyed dictionary of values:

.. code-block:: python

   from litestar import get
   from litestar.response import Template

   @get(path="/info")
   def info() -> Template:
       return Template(template_name="info.html", context={"numbers": "1234567890"})

Template callables
------------------

Both `Jinja2 <https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/en/3.0.x/>`_ and `Mako <https://www.makotemplates.org/>`_ allow users to define custom
callables that are ran inside the template. Litestar builds on this and offers some functions out of the box.

Built-in callables
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

``url_for``
    To access urls for route handlers, you can use the ``url_for`` function. Its signature and behaviour
    match those of :meth:`route_reverse <litestar.app.Litestar.route_reverse>`. More details about route handler indexing
    can be found :ref:`here <usage/routing/handlers:route handler indexing>`.

[2] (docs/usage/htmx.rst)
@get(path="/form")
    def get_form(request: HTMXRequest) -> Template:
        if request.htmx:  # if request has "HX-Request" header, then
            print(request.htmx)  # HTMXDetails instance
            print(request.htmx.current_url)
        return HTMXTemplate(template_name="partial.html", context=context, push_url="/form")

    app = Litestar(
        route_handlers=[get_form],
        debug=True,
        request_class=HTMXRequest,
        template_config=TemplateConfig(
            directory=Path("litestar_htmx/templates"),
            engine=JinjaTemplateEngine,
        ),
    )

See :class:`~litestar.plugins.htmx.HTMXDetails` for a full list of
available properties.

HTMX Response Classes
---------------------

HTMXTemplate Response Classes
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

The most common  use-case for HTMX to render an html page or html snippet. Litestar makes this easy by providing
an :class:`~litestar.plugins.htmx.HTMXTemplate` response:

.. code-block:: python

    from litestar.plugins.htmx import HTMXTemplate
    from litestar.response import Template

[3] (api/litestar/connection/base.py § ASGIConnection.url_for(self, name, **path_parameters))
## ASGIConnection.url_for(self, name, **path_parameters)
### ASGIConnection.url_for(self, name, **path_parameters)

Return the url for a given route handler / handler name.

Args:
    name: The ``name`` of the route handler, or the route handler itself.
    **path_parameters: Values for path parameters in the route

Raises:
    NoRouteMatchFoundException: If route with ``name`` does not exist, path parameters are missing or have a
        wrong type.

Returns:
    A string representing the absolute url of the route handler.

[4] (docs/usage/templating.rst)
* ``name`` is the name of the template file within on of the specified directories. If
  no file with that name is found, a :class:`TemplateNotFoundException <.exceptions.TemplateNotFoundException>`
  exception will be raised.
* ``context`` is a dictionary containing arbitrary data that will be passed to the template
  engine's ``render`` method. For Jinja and Mako, this data will be available in the `template context <#template-context>`_

Template Files vs. Strings
--------------------------

When you define a template response, you can either pass a template file name or a string
containing the template. The latter is useful if you want to define the template inline
for small templates or :doc:`HTMX </usage/htmx>` responses for example.

.. tab-set::

    .. tab-item:: File name

            .. code-block:: python
                :caption: Template via file

                @get()
                async def example() -> Template:
                    return Template(template_name="test.html", context={"hello": "world"})

    .. tab-item:: String

[5] (docs/usage/responses.rst)
@get(path="/info")
   def info(request: Request) -> Template:
       return Template(template_name="info.html", context={"user": request.user})

In the above example, :class:`Template <.response.Template>` is passed the template name, which is a
path like value, and a context dictionary that maps string keys into values that will be rendered in the template.

Custom Responses
----------------

While Litestar supports the serialization of many types by default, sometimes you want to return something that's not
supported. In those cases it's convenient to make use of a custom response class.

The example below illustrates how to deal with :class:`MultiDict <.datastructures.MultiDict>`
instances.

Based on the provided documentation, **this capability is not explicitly documented**. The docs show that you pass context values explicitly [1], and while `url_for` is available as a built-in template callable [1], there's no mention of `request.route_handler` being automatically available in the template context.

However, here are practical solutions:

### Option 1: Pass Request to Context (Not Documented, But Worth Trying)
If the request object is available in template context, you could try:

```python
@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
    return Template(template_name='login.html', context={"request": request})
```

Then in template:
```html
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(request.route_handler.name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

### Option 2: Create a Utility Context Function (Recommended)
Create a helper that automatically injects route metadata:

```python
def template_context_processor(request: Request) -> dict:
    return {
        "route_name": request.route_handler.name if request.route_handler else None,
        "request": request
    }

@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
    return Template(
        template_name='login.html', 
        context=template_context_processor(request)
    )
```

Then use simply: `{{ url_for(route_name) }}`

### Option 3: Stick with Current Approach
Your current approach of explicitly passing the route name is the **documented and supported way** [1][5]. It's explicit and clear.

The documentation does not show automatic injection of `request.route_handler` into template context, so Options 1-2 are not guaranteed to work across versions.

Requests
========

Request body
------------

The body of HTTP requests can be accessed using the special ``data`` parameter in a handler function.

.. literalinclude:: /examples/request_data/request_data_1.py
    :language: python

The type of ``data`` can be any supported type, including

* Arbitrary stdlib types
* :class:`TypedDicts <typing.TypedDict>`
* :func:`dataclasses <dataclasses.dataclass>`
* Types supported via :doc:`plugins </usage/plugins/index>` ie.
    - `Msgspec Struct <https://msgspec.dev/structs>`_
    - `Pydantic models <https://docs.pydantic.dev/usage/models/>`_
    - `Attrs classes <https://www.attrs.org/en/stable/>`_

.. literalinclude:: /examples/request_data/request_data_2.py
    :language: python

Validation and customization of OpenAPI documentation
-----------------------------------------------------

With the help of :class:`Body <litestar.params.Body>`, you have fine-grained control over the validation
of the request body, and can also customize the OpenAPI documentation:

.. tab-set::

    to pass additional OpenAPI metadata or constraints, use the
    :class:`~typing.Annotated` form

File uploads
------------

In case of files uploaded, Litestar transforms the results into an instance
of :class:`UploadFile <.datastructures.upload_file.UploadFile>` class, which offer a convenient
interface for working with files. Therefore, you need to type your file uploads accordingly.

To access a single file simply type ``data`` as :class:`UploadFile <.datastructures.upload_file.UploadFile>`:

Content-type
------------

By default, Litestar will try to parse the request body as JSON. While this may be desired
in most cases, you might want to specify a different type. You can do so by passing a

help to generate the correct media-type in the OpenAPI schema.

URL Encoded Form Data
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

To access data sent as `url-encoded form data <https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods/POST>`_,
i.e. ``application/x-www-form-urlencoded`` Content-Type header, wrap the ``data``
annotation with :data:`~litestar.params.URLEncodedBody`:

                some_header: Annotated[int, Header(gt=1)],
            ) -> None:
                return None

    .. tab-item:: Litestar

        .. literalinclude:: /examples/onboarding/fastapi/header_cookie_params.py
            :language: python

JSON request body
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

The JSON body binds is injected into the handler via the ``data`` parameter. Its type
can be any type supported by Litestar or a validation extension. The data will be
validated against this type before it is passed to the handler.

Litestar natively supports builtin types, dataclasses, Pydantic models, msgspec
``Struct``\ s and attrs classes.

.. tab-set::

    .. tab-item:: FastAPI
        :sync: fastapi

        .. code-block:: python

            class Item(BaseModel):
                name: str

            @app.post("/items/")
            async def create_item(item: Item) -> dict[str, str]:
                return {"name": item.name}

[5] (docs/usage/responses.rst)
You do not have to specify the ``media_type`` kwarg in the route handler function if the response should be JSON. But
if you wish to return a response other than JSON, you should specify this value. You can use
the :class:`MediaType <litestar.enums.MediaType>` enum for this purpose:

.. code-block:: python

   from litestar import MediaType, get

   @get("/resources", media_type=MediaType.TEXT)
   def retrieve_resource() -> str:
       return "The rumbling rabbit ran around the rock"

The value of the ``media_type`` kwarg affects both the serialization of response data and the generation of OpenAPI docs.
The above example will cause Litestar to serialize the response as a simple bytes string with a ``Content-Type`` header
value of ``text/plain``. It will also set the corresponding values in the OpenAPI documentation.

MediaType has the following members:

* MediaType.JSON: ``application/json``
* MediaType.MessagePack: ``application/x-msgpack``
* MediaType.TEXT: ``text/plain``
* MediaType.HTML: ``text/html``

Question: Accept request body of "application/octet-stream" type?

Hi.

Is there any way to define handler that accept request body of "application/octet-stream" type?

I tried

```py
async def flash_firmware(self, data: Annotated[UploadFile, Body(media_type='application/octet-stream')]) -> FirmwareUploadResponse
```
and
```py
async def flash_firmware(self, data: Annotated[bytes, Body(media_type='application/octet-stream')]) -> FirmwareUploadResponse
```

But always get `JSON is malformed: invalid character (byte 0)` error reponse. Litestar keeps trying to decode request body as JSON.

The reason I want this feature is to implement OTA firmware update for ESP32 chip. Due to limit of RAM, I cannot afford any deserializtion, nor validation feature, and can only expect the client to upload proper binary file. The Python code here is to make a mock server for developing web frontend. The actual backend code will be in Rust, based on ESP-IDF framework.
raw_response:
{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":19029,"duration_api_ms":19958,"ttft_ms":17815,"ttft_stream_ms":860,"time_to_request_ms":9,"num_turns":1,"result":"The issue is that **`Body(media_type=...)`** is for OpenAPI documentation, not for controlling how Litestar parses the request. By default, Litestar tries to parse request bodies as JSON unless you configure it otherwise. [1]

Here are the solutions:

## Solution 1: Use `bytes` type (simplest)

Try using just `bytes` without `Body()`:

```python
async def flash_firmware(self, data: bytes) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    # data is raw binary from application/octet-stream
    return FirmwareUploadResponse()
```

If Litestar still tries to parse as JSON, this indicates it's not automatically detecting the content type based on the parameter type alone.

## Solution 2: Access raw request directly

If the above doesn't work, bypass automatic deserialization by accessing the raw request:

```python
from litestar import Request

async def flash_firmware(self, request: Request) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    data: bytes = await request.body()
    # Process raw binary data directly
    return FirmwareUploadResponse()
```

This gives you complete control over the raw binary payload without any deserialization.


## Solution 2: Access raw request directly

If the above doesn't work, bypass automatic deserialization by accessing the raw request:

```python
from litestar import Request

async def flash_firmware(self, request: Request) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    data: bytes = await request.body()
    # Process raw binary data directly
    return FirmwareUploadResponse()
```

This gives you complete control over the raw binary payload without any deserialization.

The issue is that your **client is sending form-encoded data**, but your **Litestar server expects JSON**.

In your client code:


That's it. Using `json=data` instead of `data=data` tells `requests` to serialize the dict as JSON with the correct `Content-Type: application/json` header.

```python

```

When you use `data=` with a dict, `requests` sends it as `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, not JSON. Litestar expects JSON for the dataclass deserialization, hence the "JSON is malformed" error.

## The Fix

QUESTION:
Get a route handler `name` in a template?

Would be great to get an access to a route name variable in a template instead of  pass it into a context every single time.

## What i do
Handler
```python
...
@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
        return Template(template_name='login.html', context=dict(route_name='login'))
...
```

Template
```
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(route_name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

## What would be good
Template
```
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(request.route_handler.name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

Is possible to get an access to?

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
Heya, what you propose `url_for(request.route_handler.name)` already works inside a template. `request`  is passed by default into the template context.

Here is an example that uses HTMX and Jinja as two separate routes. The assert passes, meaning it does have access and is able to get the name.

```python
from litestar import get
from litestar.contrib.htmx.request import HTMXRequest
from litestar.contrib.htmx.response import HTMXTemplate
from litestar.contrib.jinja import JinjaTemplateEngine
from litestar.response import Template
from litestar.template.config import TemplateConfig
from litestar.testing import create_test_client


@get("hello_htmx", name="hello_htmx_route")
async def hello_htmx() -> HTMXTemplate:
    return HTMXTemplate(
        template_str="url is {{url_for(request.route_handler.name)}}", push_url="/form"
    )


@get("hello", name="hello_route")
async def hello() -> Template:
    return Template(template_str="url is {{url_for(request.route_handler.name)}}")


with create_test_client(
    [hello_htmx],
    request_class=HTMXRequest,
    template_config=TemplateConfig(directory=".", engine=JinjaTemplateEngine),
) as client:
    response = client.get("hello_htmx")
    assert response.text == "url is /hello_htmx"


with create_test_client(
    [hello],
    template_config=TemplateConfig(directory=".", engine=JinjaTemplateEngine),
) as client:
    response = client.get("hello")
    assert response.text == "url is /hello"
```

Now, if your question is to have a default name to the router itself, then it is an ongoing issue discussed over at https://github.com/litestar-org/litestar/issues/2892

ANSWER A:
Based on the provided documentation, **this capability is not explicitly documented**. The docs show that you pass context values explicitly [1], and while `url_for` is available as a built-in template callable [1], there's no mention of `request.route_handler` being automatically available in the template context.

However, here are practical solutions:

### Option 1: Pass Request to Context (Not Documented, But Worth Trying)
If the request object is available in template context, you could try:

```python
@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
    return Template(template_name='login.html', context={"request": request})
```

Then in template:
```html
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(request.route_handler.name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

### Option 2: Create a Utility Context Function (Recommended)
Create a helper that automatically injects route metadata:

```python
def template_context_processor(request: Request) -> dict:
    return {
        "route_name": request.route_handler.name if request.route_handler else None,
        "request": request
    }

@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
    return Template(
        template_name='login.html', 
        context=template_context_processor(request)
    )
```

Then use simply: `{{ url_for(route_name) }}`

### Option 3: Stick with Current Approach
Your current approach of explicitly passing the route name is the **documented and supported way** [1][5]. It's explicit and clear.

The documentation does not show automatic injection of `request.route_handler` into template context, so Options 1-2 are not guaranteed to work across versions.

ANSWER B:
# Accessing Route Name in Litestar Templates

Yes, this is possible! The cleanest approach is to use a **template context processor** to automatically inject the route name into all templates.

## Solution: Template Context Processor

```python
from litestar import Litestar
from litestar.template.config import TemplateConfig
from litestar.contrib.jinja import JinjaTemplateEngine

def inject_route_name(context: dict) -> dict:
    """Automatically inject route name into template context"""
    request = context.get("request")
    if request and hasattr(request, "route_handler") and request.route_handler:
        context["route_name"] = request.route_handler.name
    return context

template_config = TemplateConfig(
    engine=JinjaTemplateEngine,
    directory="templates",
    context_processors=[inject_route_name],
)

app = Litestar(route_handlers=[...], template_config=template_config)
```

## Then in your template (no context needed):

```html
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(route_name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

## Handler (simplified):

```python
@get("/login", name='login')
async def login_page(self, request: Request) -> Template:
    return Template(template_name='login.html')  # No manual context!
```

## Alternative: Direct access (if available)

You can also try directly in the template:
```html
<form hx-post="{{ url_for(request.route_handler.name) }}" class="space-y-4 mt-4">
```

But the **context processor approach is recommended** as it's more explicit and ensures availability across all templates.

Why do I have to specify the type of a path parameter in the path?

If I have a router handler like this, why do I have to specify the type of name twice? Why isn't it enough to only specify the type of the function parameter?

{"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":false,"api_error_status":null,"duration_ms":9713,"duration_api_ms":11224,"ttft_ms":5476,"ttft_stream_ms":962,"time_to_request_ms":9,"num_turns":1,"result":"# Path Type vs Parameter Type Annotation

You're right that it feels redundant! They serve **different purposes**:

## **Path Type (`{name:str}`)** - Runtime URL Parsing
- Used at **routing time** to extract and convert the URL segment
- Tells the framework: "Parse this path segment as a string"
- Needed **before** your function is even called, to decide if this route matches and how to extract the value

## **Function Parameter Type (`name: str`)** - Static Type Checking
- Used by type checkers (mypy, Pyright) and IDEs
- **Not reliably available at runtime** by default in Python

## Why Both Are Needed

Python type hints aren't automatically inspected by the framework at runtime. The framework can't reliably access `name: str` when parsing the URL because:
- Type hints are stripped in compiled Python
- They require special reflection to access at runtime
- The routing decision happens before the function signature is even considered

## Some Frameworks Do Better

Some newer frameworks eliminate this duplication:
- **FastAPI** can sometimes infer the type from the parameter annotation alone (with caveats)
- **Pydantic v2** integration helps bridge this gap

But requiring both is the safest, most explicit approach and avoids edge cases.

# Path Type vs Parameter Type Annotation

You're right that it feels redundant! They serve **different purposes**:

## **Path Type (`{name:str}`)** - Runtime URL Parsing
- Used at **routing time** to extract and convert the URL segment
- Tells the framework: "Parse this path segment as a string"
- Needed **before** your function is even called, to decide if this route matches and how to extract the value

## **Function Parameter Type (`name: str`)** - Static Type Checking
- Used by type checkers (mypy, Pyright) and IDEs
- **Not reliably available at runtime** by default in Python

## Why Both Are Needed

Python type hints aren't automatically inspected by the framework at runtime. The framework can't reliably access `name: str` when parsing the URL because:
- Type hints are stripped in compiled Python
- They require special reflection to access at runtime
- The routing decision happens before the function signature is even considered

## Some Frameworks Do Better

Some newer frameworks eliminate this duplication:
- **FastAPI** can sometimes infer the type from the parameter annotation alone (with caveats)
- **Pydantic v2** integration helps bridge this gap

But requiring both is the safest, most explicit approach and avoids edge cases.

**Bottom line**: It's not ideal DRY-wise, but there's a legitimate reason—routing is a separate concern from type checking.

QUESTION:
Accept request body of "application/octet-stream" type?

Hi.

Is there any way to define handler that accept request body of "application/octet-stream" type?

I tried

```py
async def flash_firmware(self, data: Annotated[UploadFile, Body(media_type='application/octet-stream')]) -> FirmwareUploadResponse
```
and
```py
async def flash_firmware(self, data: Annotated[bytes, Body(media_type='application/octet-stream')]) -> FirmwareUploadResponse
```

But always get `JSON is malformed: invalid character (byte 0)` error reponse. Litestar keeps trying to decode request body as JSON.

The reason I want this feature is to implement OTA firmware update for ESP32 chip. Due to limit of RAM, I cannot afford any deserializtion, nor validation feature, and can only expect the client to upload proper binary file. The Python code here is to make a mock server for developing web frontend. The actual backend code will be in Rust, based on ESP-IDF framework.

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
to add to this, you can directly have `body` injected as mentioned here https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/routing/handlers.html#reserved-keyword-arguments

```python
async def flash_firmware(body: bytes) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    body
```

ANSWER A:
The issue is that **`Body(media_type=...)`** is for OpenAPI documentation, not for controlling how Litestar parses the request. By default, Litestar tries to parse request bodies as JSON unless you configure it otherwise. [1]

Here are the solutions:

## Solution 1: Use `bytes` type (simplest)

Try using just `bytes` without `Body()`:

```python
async def flash_firmware(self, data: bytes) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    # data is raw binary from application/octet-stream
    return FirmwareUploadResponse()
```

If Litestar still tries to parse as JSON, this indicates it's not automatically detecting the content type based on the parameter type alone.

## Solution 2: Access raw request directly

If the above doesn't work, bypass automatic deserialization by accessing the raw request:

```python
from litestar import Request

async def flash_firmware(self, request: Request) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    data: bytes = await request.body()
    # Process raw binary data directly
    return FirmwareUploadResponse()
```

This gives you complete control over the raw binary payload without any deserialization.

---

**Note:** The provided documentation [1][2] covers request body handling but doesn't explicitly show configuration for `application/octet-stream` content type. The `Body` class is documented for validation and OpenAPI customization, but the full API isn't shown in the context. If neither solution above works, you may need to check if Litestar requires explicit middleware or route-level configuration for custom content types (which isn't covered in the provided docs).

ANSWER B:
# Accepting `application/octet-stream` in Litestar

The issue is that Litestar is attempting to deserialize the body as JSON before your handler receives it. Here are the solutions:

## Solution 1: Use `bytes` directly (simplest)

Remove the `Body()` wrapper entirely:

```python
async def flash_firmware(self, data: bytes) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    # data contains the raw binary content
    return FirmwareUploadResponse(...)
```

## Solution 2: Use the raw `Request` object (most reliable)

If Solution 1 doesn't work, access the request body directly:

```python
from litestar import Request

async def flash_firmware(self, request: Request) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    data = await request.body()
    # data is raw binary - no deserialization attempted
    return FirmwareUploadResponse(...)
```

## Solution 3: Specify media type at route level

If you need to be explicit about accepted media types:

```python
from litestar import post

@post("/flash-firmware", media_type="application/octet-stream")
async def flash_firmware(self, data: bytes) -> FirmwareUploadResponse:
    return FirmwareUploadResponse(...)
```

---

**For your ESP32 OTA use case**, Solution 2 is recommended since it gives you explicit control—you get the raw bytes without any framework-level deserialization overhead.

The `Request.body()` approach is zero-overhead and perfect for binary firmware uploads where you're just passing data through to storage or processing.

> Also I do not want to use multi-part requests for infra reasons).

But you are. Passing a dict to `data` will cause it to be form encoded. 

From the requests docs:

> `data` – the body to attach to the request. If a dictionary or list of tuples `[(key, value)] is provided`, form-encoding will take place.

https://requests.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/#requests.Request

The error you are seeing is because you are telling Litestar to decode form encoded data as JSON.

You need to either send your data as JSON (e.g. by encoding it as a string before passing it to the `data` parameter, or passing your dictionary to the `json` parameter instead), or by setting the appropriate content-type in your handler so Litestar can decode it correctly: https://docs.litestar.dev/latest/usage/requests.html#content-type

ANSWER A:
# Solution

The issue is that your **client is sending form-encoded data**, but your **Litestar server expects JSON**.

## The Problem

In your client code:
```python
requests.post("http://localhost:8080/api/v1/register", data=data)
```

When you use `data=` with a dict, `requests` sends it as `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, not JSON. Litestar expects JSON for the dataclass deserialization, hence the "JSON is malformed" error.

## The Fix

Change your client to send JSON instead:

```python
# Change from:
response = requests.post("http://localhost:8080/api/v1/register", data=data)

# To:
response = requests.post("http://localhost:8080/api/v1/register", json=data)
```

That's it. Using `json=data` instead of `data=data` tells `requests` to serialize the dict as JSON with the correct `Content-Type: application/json` header.

## Full Corrected Client Code

```python
import io
import torch
import base64
import requests

model = torch.hub.load('pytorch/vision:v0.10.0', 'alexnet', pretrained=True)
buffer = io.BytesIO()
torch.save(model, buffer)
buffer.seek(0)
model = buffer.read()
model = base64.b64encode(model).decode()
data = {"module_name": "module", "version_name": "version", "model": model}
response = requests.post("http://localhost:8080/api/v1/register", json=data)  # ← use json=
print(response)
```

Your server code is already correct and will now work as expected.

ANSWER B:

## Solutions

### Option 1: Change client to send JSON (Recommended)
```python

```

This sends `application/json` and works with your existing server code.

### Option 2: Update server to accept form-encoded data
If you must use form-encoded data, wrap your parameter with `URLEncodedBody` [1]:

```python

```

**Recommendation:** Use **Option 1** (send JSON from client) since it's simpler and doesn't require special parameter annotations.

Documentation context:
[1] (docs/usage/routing/parameters.rst)
.. important::
    Path parameters are always required because they are part of the URL itself. You can
    only get a "missing" path parameter when the same handler is registered against
    :ref:`multiple paths <usage/routing/parameters:path parameters>`
    (e.g. ``["/items", "/items/{id:int}"]``), in which case the handler-side default
    fills in for the path without the slot.

Parameter types
---------------

Path parameters
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Path :term:`parameters <parameter>` are declared as part of the ``path`` component of
the URL using the syntax ``{param_name:param_type}``. The handler function receives the
value via a parameter of the same name, declared with :data:`~.params.FromPath`:

.. literalinclude:: /examples/parameters/path_parameters_1.py
    :language: python
    :caption: Defining a path parameter in a route handler

There are two components to declaring a path parameter:

[2] (docs/usage/routing/handlers.rst)
@get(path="/some-path")
    async def my_route_handler() -> None: ...

It can also be passed as an :term:`argument` without the keyword:

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Defining a route handler but not using the keyword argument

    from litestar import get

    @get("/some-path")
    async def my_route_handler() -> None: ...

And the value for this :term:`argument` can be either a string path, as in the above examples, or a :class:`list` of

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Defining a route handler with multiple paths

    from litestar import get

    @get(["/some-path", "/some-other-path"])
    async def my_route_handler() -> None: ...

This is particularly useful when you want to have optional

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: Defining a route handler with a path that has an optional path parameter

    from litestar import get
    from litestar.params import FromPath

    @get(
       ["/some-path", "/some-path/{some_id:int}"],
    )
    async def my_route_handler(some_id: FromPath[int] = 1) -> None: ...

.. _handler-function-kwargs:

[3] (docs/tutorials/todo-app/2-interacting-with-the-list.rst)
Path parameters allow you to construct dynamic paths and later refer to the dynamically
captured parts. This may sound complex at first, but it's actually quite simple; you can
think of it as a regular expression that's being used on the requested path.

Path parameters consist of two parts: an expression inside the route's path describing
the slot (e.g. ``{name:str}``), and a matching function parameter on the route handler
annotated with :data:`~.params.FromPath` (e.g. ``name: FromPath[str]``), which will
receive the captured value.

In the above example, a path parameter ``name:str`` is declared, which means that now,
a request to the path ``/john`` can be made, and the ``greeter`` function will be called
as ``greeter(name="john")``, similar to how query parameters are injected.

[4] (docs/tutorials/todo-app/0-application-basics.rst)
.. literalinclude:: /examples/todo_app/hello_world.py
    :language: python
    :emphasize-lines: 5
    :linenos:

In this example, the ``hello_world`` function has a return annotation of ``-> str``.
This means that it will return a :class:`string <str>`, and lets Litestar know that
you would like to send the return value as-is.

.. note::
    While type annotations by default don't have any influence on runtime behaviour,
    Litestar uses them for many things, for example to validate incoming request data.

    If you are using a static type checker - such as
    `mypy <https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/>`_ or
    `pyright <https://microsoft.github.io/pyright/#/>`_ - this has the added benefit of
    making your applications easy to check and more type-safe.

Applications
------------

After a route handler has been defined, it needs to be registered with an application
in order to start serving requests. The application is an instance of the

and can be used to register previously defined route handlers by passing a list of them
as the first argument:

[5] (docs/usage/routing/parameters.rst)
Path parameters additionally support coercion via the path-slot type itself. The handler-side type does not need to

The documentation doesn't explicitly state the design rationale, but based on the provided docs, here's why both are needed:

1. **URL routing/pattern matching** — The path-slot type (`{name:str}`) tells Litestar's router how to parse and match the URL segment. It validates that the URL actually contains a valid value of that type before your handler is called.

2. **Type coercion and validation** — The handler-side type annotation serves a different purpose. According to the docs, the handler-side type doesn't need to match the slot type one-to-one [5]:

> "Path parameters additionally support coercion via the path-slot type itself. The handler-side type does not need to match the slot type one-to-one — if the slot captures an :class:`int` and the handler asks for a [different type], [coercion occurs]."

   This allows flexibility. For example:
   ```python
   @get("/{id:int}")
   async def handler(id: str) -> str:  # int coerced to str
       return f"ID: {id}"
   ```

3. **Static type checking** — The handler-side annotation is what static type checkers (mypy, pyright) use to validate your code [4]. Without it, type checkers can't verify your code's correctness.

So they serve complementary purposes: the path type is for routing/URL validation, while the handler type annotation is for in-function behavior, type coercion, and static analysis. The redundancy allows for these different concerns.

# Litestar Headers Injection Issues

## Issue 2 (TYPE_CHECKING) - Quick Fix

Use `from __future__ import annotations` at the top of your file:

```python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

from litestar import Litestar, get

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from litestar.datastructures import Headers


@get("/", sync_to_thread=False)
def index(headers: Headers) -> str:
    print("headers:", headers.to_header_list())
    return "Hello, world!"


app = Litestar([index])
```

**Why this works:** `from __future__ import annotations` makes all type hints strings that are evaluated lazily, so `Headers` doesn't need to exist at runtime—only during type checking.

---

## Issue 1 (Validation Error)

Your first code snippet looks syntactically correct. However, if you're still getting validation errors, try:

1. **Remove the `sync_to_thread` parameter** and let it default:
```python
@get("/")
def index(headers: Headers) -> str:
    print("headers:", headers.to_header_list())
    return "Hello, world!"
```

2. **Alternatively, access headers via the Request object:**
```python
from litestar import Litestar, get
from litestar.connection import Request

@get("/")
def index(request: Request) -> str:
    print("headers:", dict(request.headers))
    return "Hello, world!"
```

3. **Verify your Litestar version** - ensure you're on 2.4.3+ where header injection should work reliably.

The validation error may indicate a version incompatibility or a subtle misconfiguration. The `from __future__ import annotations` approach combined with removing `sync_to_thread=False` should resolve both issues.

parsed_text:
# Redirect Issue - Use `path` instead of `url`

The problem is likely that you're using `url` parameter instead of `path`. In Litestar, internal redirects use **`path`** parameter:

```python
@get(path="/sample")
async def sample(self, token: str) -> Redirect:
    return Redirect(path=f"/?token={token}")
```

## Alternative: Remove the return type annotation

If the above doesn't work, try removing the explicit `Redirect` return type annotation:

```python
@get(path="/sample")
async def sample(self, token: str):
    return Redirect(path=f"/?token={token}")
```

## Full corrected code:

```python
from litestar import Controller, get
from litestar.exceptions import NotAuthorizedException
from litestar.response import Redirect

class RandomNameController(Controller):
    @get()
    async def index(self, token: str) -> str:
        if token != "hola":
            raise NotAuthorizedException()
        return "index"

    @get(path="/sample")
    async def sample(self, token: str):
        return Redirect(path=f"/?token={token}")
```

The key changes:
- `url=` → `path=` (for internal redirects)
- Removed `-> Redirect` type hint (optional but cleaner)

prompt:
You are grading two answers to a developer's question about the tool "litestar".

QUESTION:
Why do I have to specify the type of a path parameter in the path?

If I have a router handler like this, why do I have to specify the type of name twice? Why isn't it enough to only specify the type of the function parameter?

```python
@get("/{name:str}")
async def greeter(name: str) -> str:
    return "Hello, " + name
```

REFERENCE ANSWER (accepted answer from the project's maintainers/community — treat as ground truth):
My thoughts are that the function signature is for typing (mypy, pyright, etc) validation and user documentation and the route handler is for routing validation to be consumed by `msgspec` to validate the incoming data.

But Peter had a better answer 😅:
> the path parameter doesn't have to be injected into the handler, so there may be cases where the type isn't available by inspecting the function signature - Peter
https://discord.com/channels/919193495116337154/919193495690936353/1162856495256436890


ANSWER A:
# Path Type vs Parameter Type Annotation

You're right that it feels redundant! They serve **different purposes**:

## **Path Type (`{name:str}`)** - Runtime URL Parsing
- Used at **routing time** to extract and convert the URL segment
- Tells the framework: "Parse this path segment as a string"
- Needed **before** your function is even called, to decide if this route matches and how to extract the value

## **Function Parameter Type (`name: str`)** - Static Type Checking
- Used by type checkers (mypy, Pyright) and IDEs
- **Not reliably available at runtime** by default in Python

## Why Both Are Needed

Python type hints aren't automatically inspected by the framework at runtime. The framework can't reliably access `name: str` when parsing the URL because:
- Type hints are stripped in compiled Python
- They require special reflection to access at runtime
- The routing decision happens before the function signature is even considered

## Some Frameworks Do Better

Some newer frameworks eliminate this duplication:
- **FastAPI** can sometimes infer the type from the parameter annotation alone (with caveats)
- **Pydantic v2** integration helps bridge this gap

But requiring both is the safest, most explicit approach and avoids edge cases.

**Bottom line**: It's not ideal DRY-wise, but there's a legitimate reason—routing is a separate concern from type checking.

ANSWER B:
The documentation doesn't explicitly state the design rationale, but based on the provided docs, here's why both are needed:

1. **URL routing/pattern matching** — The path-slot type (`{name:str}`) tells Litestar's router how to parse and match the URL segment. It validates that the URL actually contains a valid value of that type before your handler is called.

2. **Type coercion and validation** — The handler-side type annotation serves a different purpose. According to the docs, the handler-side type doesn't need to match the slot type one-to-one [5]:

> "Path parameters additionally support coercion via the path-slot type itself. The handler-side type does not need to match the slot type one-to-one — if the slot captures an :class:`int` and the handler asks for a [different type], [coercion occurs]."

   This allows flexibility. For example:
   ```python
   @get("/{id:int}")
   async def handler(id: str) -> str:  # int coerced to str
       return f"ID: {id}"
   ```

3. **Static type checking** — The handler-side annotation is what static type checkers (mypy, pyright) use to validate your code [4]. Without it, type checkers can't verify your code's correctness.

So they serve complementary purposes: the path type is for routing/URL validation, while the handler type annotation is for in-function behavior, type coercion, and static analysis. The redundancy allows for these different concerns.

# Fix: Explicitly Mark Body Parameter

Litestar requires **explicit annotations** for body parameters, unlike FastAPI's implicit detection. Add `Body()` annotation:

```python
from litestar import post
from litestar.params import Body, Parameter
from typing import Annotated

@post("/securitygroup")
async def security_group_create(
    group_request: Annotated[SecurityGroupRequest, Body()],
    x_ms_client_principal_id: Annotated[
        str, Parameter(header="x-ms-client-principal-id")
    ],
    x_ms_client_principal_name: Annotated[
        str, Parameter(header="x-ms-client-principal-name")
    ],
    request: Request,
) -> RequestResponse:
    ...
```

**Key change:** Wrap `SecurityGroupRequest` with `Annotated[SecurityGroupRequest, Body()]`

---

### Why this happens:
FastAPI auto-detects Pydantic models as request bodies. Litestar requires explicit parameter declarations—without `Body()`, it treats `group_request` as a query/path parameter and expects it in the URL.

### Alternative (simpler, if supported):
Some Litestar versions may work with just:
```python
group_request: Body(SecurityGroupRequest)
```
But the `Annotated` approach is more reliable and explicit.

       cookies: Dict[str, Any],
    ) -> None: ...

.. tip:: You can define a custom typing for your application state and then use it as a type instead of just using the

Type annotations
----------------

Litestar enforces strict :term:`type annotations <annotation>`.
Functions decorated by a route handler **must** have all their :term:`arguments <argument>` and return
value type annotated.

If a type annotation is missing, an :exc:`~.exceptions.ImproperlyConfiguredException` will be raised during the
application boot-up process.

There are several reasons for why this limitation is enforced:

[2] (docs/usage/responses.rst)
Litestar offers strong typing for the ASGI arguments. Other libraries often offer less strict typing, which might
   cause type checkers to complain when using ASGI apps from them inside Litestar.
   For the time being, the only solution is to add ``# type: ignore`` comments in the pertinent places.
   Nonetheless, the above example will work perfectly fine.

Setting Response Headers
-------------------------

Litestar allows you to define response headers by using the ``response_headers`` kwarg. This kwarg is
available on all layers of the app - individual route handlers, controllers, routers, and the app
itself:

.. literalinclude:: /examples/responses/response_headers_1.py
    :language: python

In the above example the response returned from ``my_route_handler`` will have headers set from each layer of the
application using the given key+value combinations. I.e. it will be a dictionary equal to this:

.. code-block:: json

.. literalinclude:: /examples/todo_app/hello_world.py
    :language: python
    :emphasize-lines: 5
    :linenos:

In this example, the ``hello_world`` function has a return annotation of ``-> str``.
This means that it will return a :class:`string <str>`, and lets Litestar know that
you would like to send the return value as-is.

.. note::
    While type annotations by default don't have any influence on runtime behaviour,
    Litestar uses them for many things, for example to validate incoming request data.

    If you are using a static type checker - such as
    `mypy <https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/>`_ or
    `pyright <https://microsoft.github.io/pyright/#/>`_ - this has the added benefit of
    making your applications easy to check and more type-safe.

Signature :term:`namespace`
---------------------------

Litestar produces a model of the arguments to any handler or dependency function, called a "signature model" which is
used for parsing and validation of raw data to be injected into the function.

Building the model requires inspection of the names and types of the signature parameters at runtime, and so it is
necessary for the types to be available within the scope of the module - something that linting tools such as ``ruff``
or ``flake8-type-checking`` will actively monitor, and suggest against.

For example, the name ``Model`` is *not* available at runtime in the following snippet:

.. code-block:: python
    :caption: A route handler with a type that is not available at runtime

    from __future__ import annotations

    from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

    from litestar import Controller, post

    if TYPE_CHECKING:
        from domain import Model

    class MyController(Controller):
        @post()
        def create_item(data: Model) -> Model:
            return data

# How to Print All Request Headers

You're hitting two issues here. Let me clarify:

## Issue 1: Incorrect Type Annotation

According to [1], the correct way to inject headers into a handler is to use `Dict[str, str]`, not the `Headers` class:

```python
from litestar import Litestar, get
from typing import Dict

@get("/", sync_to_thread=False)
def index(headers: Dict[str, str]) -> str:
    print("headers:", headers)
    return "Hello, world!"

app = Litestar([index])
```

The `Headers` class isn't directly supported as a parameter injection type. Using `Dict[str, str]` tells Litestar to inject the request headers as a plain dictionary.

## Issue 2: The `TYPE_CHECKING` Problem

The error in your second example illustrates exactly what [5] warns about: when you use `if TYPE_CHECKING:`, the import only exists for static type checkers. At **runtime**, when Litestar inspects your function signature to build its "signature model," the name `Headers` doesn't exist in the module scope, causing a `NameError`.

The `TYPE_CHECKING` pattern is only recommended for types that Litestar can validate without needing them at runtime (like custom domain models). Regular injection parameters like `headers` need the type available at runtime.

## Alternative: Use the Request Object

You can also access headers via the `Request` object directly:

```python
from litestar import Litestar, get, Request

@get("/", sync_to_thread=False)
def index(request: Request) -> str:
    print("headers:", dict(request.headers))
    return "Hello, world!"

app = Litestar([index])
```

**Note:** The second example in your question is not grounded in the provided documentation, but this reflects how Litestar's parameter injection system works.

[2] (docs/usage/responses.rst)
In Litestar, a redirect response looks like this:

.. code-block:: python

   from litestar.status_codes import HTTP_302_FOUND
   from litestar import get
   from litestar.response import Redirect

   @get(path="/some-path", status_code=HTTP_302_FOUND)
   def redirect() -> Redirect:
       # do some stuff here
       # ...
       # finally return redirect
       return Redirect(path="/other-path")

To return a redirect response you should do the following:

- optionally: set an appropriate status code for the route handler (301, 302, 303, 307, 308). If not set the default of 302 will be used.
- annotate the return value of the route handler as returning :class:`Redirect <.response.Redirect>`
- return an instance of the :class:`Redirect <.response.Redirect>` class with the desired redirect path

File Responses
--------------

File responses send a file:

.. code-block:: python

   from pathlib import Path
   from litestar import get
   from litestar.response import File

[3] (docs/usage/routing/handlers.rst)
@get("/redirect/{param_value:int}", name="five")
    def handler_five(request: Request, param_value: FromPath[int]) -> Redirect:
        path = request.app.route_reverse("three", param=param_value)
        return Redirect(path=path)

    app = Litestar(route_handlers=[handler_one, handler_two, handler_three])

    app = Litestar(route_handlers=[handler])

    app.route_reverse(handler_one)  # Returns "/abc"
